items.title: Prefer VJPs over full Jacobians in training
items.avoid: Calling `functional.jacobian` on every layer forward pass during standard training.
items.prefer: Use reverse-mode autograd (`backward`) which implements vector-Jacobian products along the graph.
items.why: Training needs one row of the Jacobian (gradient of loss), not the entire matrix.
items.example: For y = Wx with W ∈ ℝ^{512×512}, full J has 512² entries; backprop computes Wᵀδ in O(512²) without storing J explicitly.

items.title: Check Hessian eigenvalues on low-dimensional problems first
items.avoid: Jumping to Hessian analysis on a full ResNet before understanding 2D toy surfaces.
items.prefer: Compute H for small models or scalar-parameter slices to build intuition about sharp vs flat minima.
items.why: Low-dimensional plots and eigenvalues make saddle/min classification concrete before scaling up.
items.example: Plot f(x,y)=x²+10y² and verify H=diag(2,20) explains slow progress along x vs fast along y under fixed learning rate.

items.title: Use create_graph=True for exact Hessian-vector products
items.avoid: Finite-difference Hessians on noisy mini-batch losses without controlling step size.
items.prefer: Differentiate the gradient with autograd (`grad(..., create_graph=True)`) for Hessian-vector products h ↦ Hh.
items.why: HVP costs O(p) per product vs O(p²) for full H—feasible for curvature probes in research code.
items.example: PyTorch: `grad(grad_outputs=v)` on parameters with create_graph gives Hv without forming H.
